title: Feature Engineering for NER
concept: Taggers use word features like capitalization, prefix/suffix, and neighboring words to identify entity candidates.

exercises.prompt: What kind of clues do NER taggers use to identify names, dates, and locations?

options.text: Only the length of the word
Capitalization, word endings, and neighbor words

exercises.explain: NER taggers look at surrounding words, word endings, and capital letters to understand context and label entities.

cards.title: Spotting the Clues
cards.body: How does a computer know that 'Washington' is a person in one sentence, but a place in another? It doesn't just read the word—it looks at clues around it! Let's learn how we build these clever clues.

cards.title: What is Feature Engineering for NER?
cards.body: Feature engineering for Named Entity Recognition (NER) means creating extra clues for our AI. Instead of just looking at the word itself, we feed the model helpful details like whether the word is capitalized, its prefixes/suffixes, and the words surrounding it.

cards.title: The Detective's Clues
cards.body: Imagine you are a detective looking for clues in a sentence. You don't just look at the dictionary meaning of a word. You look at its 'outfit' (Does it start with a capital letter like a name?), its 'name tags' (Does it end in -glen or -shire?), and who it is hanging out with (Is it standing next to the word 'Street' or 'Mr.'?). All these clues help you solve the mystery of what the word is!

cards.title: Clues in Action
cards.body: Let's look at the word 'Paris'. If it starts with a capital 'P', that's a feature clue! If the word before it is 'in' or 'to' (as in 'going to Paris'), that's a neighbor clue! The model combines these clues to decide that 'Paris' is a Location.

cards.title: Myth: It's Just a Dictionary!
cards.body: You might think computers only look at a giant dictionary of names to find entities. But what if a new company is started tomorrow? A dictionary would miss it! That's why models rely on structural clues like capitalization and surrounding words to detect new entities.

cards.title: Spotting the Difference
cards.body: Comparing how a model looks at raw text versus text enriched with smart features.

right.text: The model sees 'Amazon' (Capitalized) next to '.com' (Neighbor). It instantly knows it is a Company!

left.text: The model only sees 'amazon'. It cannot tell if it is a rainforest or a shopping website.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a word-ending (suffix) feature that might help an NER tagger identify a location?

options.text: Whether the sentence contains a question mark.
If the word ends in '-ton' or '-land'.
The total number of vowels in the word.
If the word is written in all lowercase letters.

exercises.explain: Suffixes like '-ton' (Boston, Kingston) or '-land' (England, Thailand) are common word endings that help the model identify locations.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The words next to a target word are useless for NER tagging.
exercises.explain: Neighboring words (like 'Mr.', 'Street', or 'Corp.') provide critical context to help the model identify what kind of entity a word is.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the rule about NER features.
exercises.sentence: Looking at whether a word starts with an uppercase letter is using the feature of ________.
exercises.explain: Capitalization is one of the most powerful visual features for finding names of people, places, and brands.

exercises.prompt: Identify the AI using smart NER features to label a word:

options.text: An AI recognizes 'Smith' as a person because it starts with a capital letter and follows the word 'Dr.'
An AI assumes 'Smith' is a verb because it has five letters.
An AI flags 'Smith' because it is spelled incorrectly.

exercises.explain: Only Option A represents a feature-based decision, using capitalization and the neighbor word 'Dr.' to identify a doctor's name. Options B and C are just random guesses or spelling checks.

exercises.prompt: You want your NER tagger to recognize 'Apple Inc.' as an organization. How does feature engineering help the model do this?

options.text: It translates the sentence into another language first.
It uses clues like capitalization (Apple), endings, and neighbors (Inc.).
It checks a giant list of every company name ever created.
It guesses completely at random based on word length.

exercises.explain: Capital letters, nearby indicator words like 'Inc.', and suffix shapes are all classic 'features' that tell an NER model a word is highly likely to be a company or organization!
